elements.text: C2-2-05, Whole Loan Purchasing Process (10/04/2023)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information on the process Fannie Mae uses to notify the lender (and the servicer, if the lender is not servicing the loan) of a whole loan purchase, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Notification to Lender of Purchases of Regularly Amortizing Mortgages
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Contents of the Purchase Advice
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reconciliation of Purchase Advices and After Sale Corrections to Whole Loan Disbursements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Submission of Mortgage Record Change to HUD for FHA Mortgage Purchases
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Notification to Lender of Purchases of Regularly Amortizing Mortgages
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders can access the following documents in Fannie Mae Connect:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a seller's Purchase Advice to detail Fannie Mae's disbursement of the purchase proceeds, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a report to summarize the lender's purchases for each whole loan commitment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Servicers can obtain a Purchase Advice to provide the information necessary for its accounting records for the mortgages that it is servicing on behalf of a lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Contents of the Purchase Advice
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The Purchase Advice will contain the following information:
elements.label: text

elements.text: each loan, identified by a Fannie Mae loan number (which the servicer should enter into its records immediately because it must be used in reporting all subsequent activity related to the mortgage to Fannie Mae);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: details about the loan purchase, including loan information, price adjustments, and purchase proceeds; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a report with the applicable payee code for each wire transfer request.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The Purchase Advice will be available on the business day Fannie Mae funds the purchase proceeds.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Reconciliation of Purchase Advices and After Sale Corrections to Whole Loan Disbursements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender should examine each Purchase Advice it receives and reconcile the advice to its books and records to ensure that any transfer of funds is properly credited to the lender's designated bank.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae expects lenders to have appropriate controls and procedures in place to perform timely and accurate reconciliations of the transfers of funds for all whole loan deliveries.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender must submit a post-purchase adjustment request to provide notice of any error, omission, or irregularity in the transfer of funds or in processing the request for the repetitive transfer within 30 days of the date of the Purchase Advice. Otherwise, Fannie Mae will assume that the information on its Purchase Advice is correct.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the lender believes the amount disbursed is incorrect, it can request an adjustment by following the instructions provided in Seller/Servicer-Initiated Post-Purchase Adjustments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The request must include the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the lender's nine-digit Fannie Mae seller/servicer number,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the ten-digit Fannie Mae loan number,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the Post-Purchase Adjustment Request form, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: applicable documentation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See Seller/Servicer-Initiated Post-Purchase Adjustments in C1-2-02, Loan Data and Documentation Delivery Requirements for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Submission of Mortgage Record Change to HUD for FHA Mortgage Purchases
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: After the lender receives notification that Fannie Mae has purchased a regularly amortizing FHA loan for its portfolio, the lender must complete a Mortgage Record Change (HUD Form 92080) and submit it to HUD through FHA Connection.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For notifications to FHA, lenders will also need to include Fannie Mae's Mortgagee Number 950010999 on the form. Only the first five digits of this number are required if submitting the HUD form through FHA Connection.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Fannie Mae is not currently purchasing FHA loans.

elements.text: C2-2-06, Authorization to Transfer Funds (05/01/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic explains how a lender authorizes Fannie Mae to transfer funds for whole loan deliveries into its designated accounts, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender's Agreement Related to the Execution of the Authorization to Transfer Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Authorization for Repetitive Transfers
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Changes to or Deletion of the Authority to Transfer Funds
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Documentation Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae will not transfer purchase proceeds for whole loan deliveries to a lender's designated bank account until the lender has provided Fannie Mae with an executed Certificate of Authority, Incumbency, and Specimen Signatures ( Form 360) that designates the person or persons authorized to establish, change, or delete wire transfer instructions on the lender's behalf.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lender's Agreement Related to the Execution of the Authorization to Transfer Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: By executing  Form 360, a lender agrees that
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae is authorized to conclusively rely on the accuracy, genuineness, and good faith of any written communication related to transfer instructions that bears the signature of one of the individuals designated on Form 360; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: it will be fully responsible for any and all losses incurred by Fannie Mae that result from Fannie Mae's reliance on any instruction given to Fannie Mae by the lender's authorized representatives or any other person who has (or obtains) access to information or documents that compromise the security of any Fannie Mae electronic fund transfer systems or processes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: After Fannie Mae receives an executed Form 360 from a lender, Fannie Mae will rely on the information on that Certification until such time as the lender requests to modify or delete it.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Authorization for Repetitive Transfers
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A repetitive transfer is one in which all aspects of the transfer, other than the date of the transfer and the amount of funds transferred, remain constant over time.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A lender that wants the purchase proceeds for the whole loan deliveries that it submits over any period of time to be wired to the same account(s) and depository institution(s) must have its authorized representative(s) submit a separate Seller's Designation of Wire Transfer Instructions ( Form 482) for each arrangement, specifying any desired transfer routing instructions, any intermediary depository institutions, and appropriate account numbers for these repetitive transfers. Fannie Mae will
elements.label: text

elements.text: call the authorized representative who signed the Form 482 request to confirm validity prior to processing.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: assign a unique payee code to identify each of the lender's instructions for repetitive transfers. The lender must then use this payee code to identify each whole loan delivery for which the purchase proceeds are to be wired to the designated payee.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: rely on the ABA routing number and the account numbers that the lender's representative specifies on the Form 482 when it creates a payee code to identify each individual payee arrangement. Fannie Mae will make no effort to verify the accuracy of the routing number or the account numbers.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Changes to or Deletion of the Authority to Transfer Funds
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: To add, delete, or change an authorized representative, the lender must submit a new Form 360 and corporate resolution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae reserves the right to verify or authenticate any request for a change, but the lender cannot consider Fannie Mae's failure to do so an act of negligence.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: In emergency situations, Fannie Mae may, in its sole discretion, delete the name of an authorized representative on the basis of a telephone call and/or an email request from another authorized representative; however, a revised  Form 360 and a new corporate resolution must then be submitted promptly as confirmation of the change.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae will make reasonable efforts to act quickly on requests for emergency deletions of the names of authorized representatives. Fannie Mae cannot guarantee that it will be able to act quickly enough to prevent the execution of transfers by the individuals being deleted and bears no liability for the execution of any such transfers.

elements.text: Subpart C3, Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This subpart describes Fannie Mae's requirements for pooling mortgages that will serve as the underlying asset for MBS, which includes UMBS. It includes Fannie Mae's MBS program parameters and other information regarding MBS commitments, guaranty and buyup and buydown fees, pooling loans into fixed-rate and ARM MBS and Fannie Majors, and delivering and trading MBS and UMBS.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter C3-1, MBS Program Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter provides basic information on Fannie Mae's MBS program and the lender's responsibilities associated with pooling loans into MBS, which includes UMBS.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-1-01, General Information About Fannie Mae's MBS Program (10/06/2021)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic includes general information about Fannie Mae's MBS program (which includes UMBS), including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Program Description
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: UMBS Program Description
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: MBS Single-Family Pool Trust Agreement
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Guaranty Fees
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Pricing Parameters
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Servicing Fees
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Remittance Types and Cycles
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Program Transaction Options
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Structured Transactions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae's Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Program Description
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae MBS are securities that represent an ownership interest in pools of residential mortgages with similar characteristics. MBS are also known as 'pass-through certificates,' because the principal and interest on the underlying mortgages are passed through to investors based on the percentage of the security that each investor owns. Fannie Mae guarantees to the related trust holding the mortgages that it will supplement amounts received by that trust as required to permit timely payment of principal and interest on the MBS.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae's MBS program provides for the issuance and sale of MBS that represent fractional, undivided, beneficial ownership interests in a distinct pool of mortgages, such as the following mortgage types:
elements.label: text

elements.text: conventional,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: FHA-insured,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: VA-guaranteed, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: HUD-guaranteed Section 184 loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS transactions result in the formation of one of two types of pools:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a single-lender pool, in which all of the mortgages share a common characteristic, such as amortization type, loan term or range of loan terms, mortgage type, or ARM plan number. The minimum pool size (aggregate UPB) for a fixed rate, single-lender pool is $1 million. For ARMs, the minimum pool size is $500,000.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a multiple-lender pool, known as a Fannie Majors, that consists of pools of whole mortgages delivered by more than one lender. For Fannie Mae to open a Fannie Majors pool, the pool's aggregate UPB must meet the minimum amounts noted above. Once opened, a lender may deliver loans with UPBs as low as $1,000.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: UMBS Program Description
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A common security issued by the GSEs that is eligible for trading in the To-be-Announced market. The securities are backed by fixed-rate loans secured by single-family one-to-four unit properties and are identified by certain pool prefixes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Single-Family Pool Trust Agreement
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Fannie Mae holds, in its capacity as trustee, the mortgages sold to Fannie Mae by a lender or lenders in a trust comprising the pool and issues MBS that are backed by those mortgages. All mortgage loans related to a pool represent a separate trust and issuance of MBS. For each issuance of MBS, there will be an issue supplement to the Trust Agreement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The Trust Agreement and any issue supplement are entered into by Fannie Mae in its corporate capacity (as Issuer, Master Servicer and Guarantor) and as Trustee. The trust agreement is posted at Single Family Master Trust Agreement. Issue supplements are available to investors in related pools upon request from the FixedIncome Investor Helpline; see  E-1-02, List of Contacts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The Amended and Restated 2016 Single-Family Master Trust Agreement is effective for all fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgage loans in pools delivered to Fannie Mae, and Pooled from Portfolio loans included in pools, for all MBS issued on or after June 1, 2016.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Guaranty Fees
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must pay Fannie Mae a guaranty fee remittance each month as compensation for the lender's right to participate in the MBS program. The guaranty fee is ultimately a corporate responsibility of the servicer and is not a function of the pool cash flows; therefore, it must be paid even if there is no pool collection activity.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The guaranty fee remittance rate for MBS mortgages varies depending on:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the credit risk of mortgages included in the pool,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the servicing option that applies to each mortgage in the pool, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the remittance cycle that applies to the pool.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For additional details, see C1-1-01, Execution Options.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders may buy up or buy down their guaranty fees. See  C3-2-03, MBS Remittance Type and Selecting a Remittance Cycle, and Chapter C3-3, MBS Guaranty Fees and Guaranty Fee Buyups and Buydowns , for more information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Pricing Parameters
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: MBS prices are driven by investors and can change continually throughout the day. Pricing is a function of the security itself, such as, the type of mortgage backing the security and the coupon rate (the rate at which interest is paid to the investor); and market factors, such as interest rate fluctuations and overall MBS demand.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS may be bought or sold at par, at a premium, or at a discount, that is, a price equal to, greater than, or less than 100% of their face value.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Servicing Fees
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender determines the amount of the total servicing compensation it wants to receive when it establishes the interest rate for the mortgage, but Fannie Mae requires that it provide for at least a minimum servicing fee. Generally, the total servicing fee for a regularly amortizing mortgage is the difference between the interest rate of a loan and the sum of (a) the pass-through rate on the loan (or, for ARMs, the loan's contributions to the pool accrual rate) and (b) the guaranty fee.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The total servicing fee that is established when the MBS pool is formed generally will remain in effect for the life of the pool (without regard to whether the pool contains fixed-rate loans or ARMs).
elements.label: text

elements.text: For all fixed-rate loans, the minimum servicing fee is 25 basis points (.25%) and the maximum servicing fee is limited to 50 basis points (.50%).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For ARM loans, the minimum servicing fee is 25 basis points (.25%).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For additional information regarding minimum and maximum servicing fees, refer to Servicing Guide F-2-08, Servicing Fees for MBS Mortgage Loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Remittance Types and Cycles
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The scheduled principal and interest due on mortgages pooled into MBS must be remitted to Fannie Mae, whether or not it is collected from the borrowers (that is, accounted for using the scheduled/scheduled remittance type).
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Program Transaction Options
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders can opt to either hold the MBS in their investment portfolio (often referred to as a 'swap-and-hold' transaction) or sell the MBS to another investor as part of the same transaction ('swap-and-sell'). Lenders can choose to service the mortgages underlying the MBS or sell the servicing associated with these loans to another financial institution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Structured Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: MBS may be combined with other Fannie Mae mortgage-related securities to create a single structured transaction security, such as:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Supers - Pass-through securities backed by groups of existing UMBS or other existing Supers;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Megas - Pass-through securities backed by groups of existing MBS or other existing Megas;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: SMBS - Pass-through securities created by either (1) restructuring the interest and principal payments into separately tradable securities (standard SMBS) or (2) with Fannie Mae's approval, depositing into an SMBS trust a portion of the interest payable on mortgage loans backing certain MBS-the 'excess yield' (excess servicing SMBS); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: REMICs - Multiclass mortgage-related securities backed by UMBS, MBS, Supers, Megas or whole loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders that wish to deliver mortgage-related securities to Fannie Mae in exchange for a structured transaction security should contact the Capital Markets Structured Transactions group, to seek approval to do so.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae's Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lenders in good standing may be eligible to use the services of Fannie Mae's Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk ('the Desk') to either buy or sell MBS (and whole loans as well). The Desk also assists lenders in obtaining current market quotes and in finding markets for nonstandard MBS products. However, the Desk will not make individualized trade recommendations or determine the appropriateness or benefit of any particular transaction or strategy for a lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders should note that they must consent to the recordation of all telephone conversations with the Desk. In the event of a conflict between a recorded telephone conversation and a confirmation or settlement notice sent by Fannie Mae to the lender, the recorded conversation represents the official terms of the transaction.

elements.text: C3-1-02, Preparing to Pool Loans into MBS (05/01/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information on the steps lenders need to take before they can obtain MBS commitments and pool loans into MBS, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Obtaining Approval to Deliver MBS Loans
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Selecting a Document Custodian
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Obtaining Approval to Deliver MBS Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender must obtain approval for MBS execution and access to Fannie Mae's MBS committing application before it can deliver mortgages into a single-lender MBS pool or a Fannie Majors.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae considers a number of factors prior to offering MBS execution to a lender. These factors include, but are not limited to, the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the expertise of the lender's management in securitization,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: whether the lender's operational processes support MBS delivery and servicing requirements,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: the lender's ability to manage and maintain a delivery process that ensures the delivery of accurate data to Fannie Mae,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender's prepayment speeds, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the lender's performance against outstanding Fannie Mae obligations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae does not accept requests for MBS execution from lenders whose selling or servicing privileges have been suspended or that have not performed satisfactorily under other MBS commitments or contracts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Selecting a Document Custodian
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must select an eligible document custodian and have an executed Master Custodial Agreement (Form 2017) in place before obtaining an MBS commitment (see A3-3-04, Document Custodians). The document custodian certifies, takes and retains actual physical possession of the custodial documents for the mortgages. Also, see the RDC guide for document custodian eligibility criteria, and Fannie Mae's Active Document Custodians for a complete list of eligible custodians.

elements.text: C3-2-01, Determining Eligibility for Loans Pooled into MBS (09/07/2022)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic contains information on eligibility requirements for loans pooled into MBS (which includes UMBS), including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: General Eligibility Requirements for Loans Pooled into MBS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Nonstandard Loans
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Identifying Nonstandard Loans at Delivery
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Amortization Schedule Requirements for MBS Loans
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Requirements for Pooling Seasoned Loans into MBS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Interest Rate Requirements for Loans Pooled into MBS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Servicer Requirements for Loans Pooled into MBS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Eligibility Requirements for Loans Pooled into MBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All loans pooled into MBS must be secured by a first mortgage and must represent the entire right, title, and interest in the note and the related security instrument, unless the MBS commitment expressly indicates otherwise.
elements.label: text

elements.text: As of the issue date of the MBS, the loans in the related MBS pool may not be delinquent by more than one monthly installment of principal and interest that is due on the issue date (including the period beginning on the second day of the month preceding the issue date and ending on the issue date). For example, if the first payment date is November 1, and the issue date of the MBS is January 1, then, in order to be eligible for purchase by Fannie Mae, the November and December payments must have been paid, and the only payment that may be delinquent (due) would be for the period December 2 through January 1.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In addition,
elements.label: text

elements.text: if the loan is one year or less from the first payment date to the pool issue date, the borrower cannot have any 30-day delinquencies since origination; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if the current borrower assumed the loan and has owned the property for one year or less, the borrower must have no 30-day delinquencies since purchasing the property.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See  B2-1.5-02, Loan Eligibility, for the requirements concerning seasoned loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Nonstandard Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: 'Nonstandard loans' (or loans with nonstandard characteristics) may be pooled into MBS, but depending on the concentration in the pool, may only be pooled as negotiated rather than standard transactions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Nonstandard loans are:
elements.label: text

elements.text: co-op share loans,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: relocation loans (defined below),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loans with significant interest rate buydowns, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: high-balance loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In a TBA-eligible UMBS pool, nonstandard loans are each limited to 10% of the issue date UPB of a TBA-eligible UMBS pool. If loans with more than one of the nonstandard characteristics are included in the same TBA-eligible UMBS pool, the sum of the issue date UPB of two or more of the loans with nonstandard characteristics may not exceed 15% of the total issue date UPB of the pool. The 15% cumulative limitation, however, does not apply to high-balance loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: For pools with greater than 10% concentrations of nonstandard loans, see the Pool Prefix Glossary for the applicable pool prefixes. Nonstandard loans may be delivered into existing MBS commitments and may use the same base guaranty fee as those used for the lender's standard conforming loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders may deliver loans with the nonstandard characteristics described above into Fannie Majors TBA-eligible UMBS pools. For Fannie Majors requirements, see C3-6-01, Parameters for Pooling Loans Into Fannie Majors.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For the purposes of TBA pooling parameters, a relocation loan is an owner-occupied purchase money loan, originated pursuant to an established employee relocation program, administered by the employer (or its agent), where the employer relocates employees as part of its normal course of business. A loan that involves an employee relocation that does not meet these parameters is not considered a relocation loan for TBA pooling purposes, and as such, the pooling limitations and SFC 013 delivery requirement are not applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The issue date UPB of loans with significant interest rate buydowns and the issue date UPB of co-op share loans individually may not exceed 10% of the total issue date UPB of the TBA-eligible UMBS, and together may not exceed 15% of the total TBA-eligible UMBS. However, while the issue date UPB of high-balance loans and the issue date UPB of co-op share loans individually may not exceed 10% of the total issue date UPB of the TBAeligible UMBS, together they may exceed 15% of the total TBA-eligible UMBS because high-balance loans are not subject to the 15% limitation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Identifying Nonstandard Loans at Delivery
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: As a reminder, nonstandard loans must be identified at delivery with the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: relocation loans - SFC 013
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loans with significant interest rate buydowns - SFC 014
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: high-balance loans - SFC 808
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: co-op share loans - the Legal Structure in Loan Delivery is 'cooperative.'
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Amortization Schedule Requirements for MBS Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For loans pooled into MBS, the first payment date must be no later than two months from the final disbursement date of the loan proceeds. In the case of a single-close construction-to-permanent loan, the two-month period begins at the time of the conversion to permanent financing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following table provides an example of this requirement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the final disbursement date occurs in...
elements.label: text

elements.text: Then the first payment date must be no later than...
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If the final disbursement date occurs in...
elements.label: text

elements.text: Then the first payment date must be no later than...
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: January
elements.label: text

elements.text: March
elements.label: text

elements.text: February
elements.label: text

elements.text: April
elements.label: text

elements.text: November
elements.label: text

elements.text: January
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standard pooling allows lenders to include in an MBS pool only those loans for which the first payment date is no later than the first day of the month that immediately follows the issue date of the related MBS. The pool issue date is the first day of the month in which securities backed by the MBS pool are issued. For seasoned loans, the first payment date must be at least 12 months prior to the pool's issue date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In addition, the loan's amortization schedule must not provide for any period during which principal has been disbursed and is outstanding, but interest is not accruing. However, if the lender chooses to pool within the same month of loan origination (also known as "same month pooling"), the loan may begin to amortize on the first day of the month that is two months after the issue date of the securities, with the initial remittance to Fannie Mae being an 'interest-only' remittance, because the borrower will not have made their first payment at the time of the initial remittance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A negotiated contract is needed to pool loans for which the borrower's monthly payment is due on a day other than first of the month ('odd due dates').
elements.label: text

elements.text: Requirements for Pooling Seasoned Loans into MBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders may pool current or seasoned loans into a single-lender MBS. Fannie Mae has no minimum seasoning requirement for conventional loans included in single pool transactions. Refer to C3-6-01, Parameters for Pooling Loans Into Fannie Majors for additional information about seasoning for Fannie Majors.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a pool of ARM loans will have amortized by more than 12 monthly payments as of the issue date of the pool, the terms of a negotiated contract will specify whether the loans will be treated as current or seasoned. The key determinants are the length of the interest rate adjustment intervals and whether interest rate or payment adjustments will have occurred by the pool's issue date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For a converted ARM (which is a fixed-rate loan that was once an ARM until the borrower exercised an option to convert it to a fixed rate), the 12-month period is measured from the date of conversion to the issue date month of the related pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: To sell seasoned loans under an MBS execution, lenders must have an MBS commitment that permits delivery of seasoned loans. For information on pricing and parameters, contact the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk (see  E-1-02, List of Contacts).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Interest Rate Requirements for Loans Pooled into MBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Loans with different annual rates of interest can be included in the same MBS pool, as long as those rates fall within the minimum and maximum spreads Fannie Mae allows between the loan interest rates and the passthrough rate for the MBS pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For fixed-rate loans that are pooled, the minimum allowable interest rate is 25 basis points (.25%) above the pool's pass-through rate and the maximum allowable interest rate is 112.5 basis points (1.125%) above the pool's pass-through rate.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Generally, for ARMs that are pooled, the minimum allowable interest rate is the sum of the lowest guaranty fee (after all applicable adjustments have been made, including buyups and buydowns) and the lender's minimum servicing fee, which must include renewal premiums for lender-purchased mortgage insurance, if applicable. The recommended maximum allowable interest rate is 100 basis points (1%) over the minimum allowable interest rate. Lenders must comply with the allowable minimum and maximum interest rates for each MBS ARM structure.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Servicer Requirements for Loans Pooled into MBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All loans in a single issuer MBS pool must be serviced by the same entity. If a lender plans to deliver several pieces for inclusion in a multiple pool (Fannie Majors), each piece may have a different servicer. The lender may deliver any combination of seller/servicer numbers for inclusion in a multiple pool (Fannie Majors), however the servicers designated must be servicing loans in the multiple pool that have the same remittance cycle as the loans being delivered.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-2-02, Selecting a Servicing Option (04/01/2009)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information about the two options lenders must choose from when deciding how they wish to service loans pooled into MBS. These options include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Impact of Servicing Option Selection on Guaranty Fees
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Regular Servicing Option
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Special Servicing Option
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Impact of Servicing Option Selection on Guaranty Fees
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Guaranty fees are affected by the servicing option chosen by the lender. Generally, the guaranty fee for pooled loans serviced under the Regular Servicing option is typically lower than for pooled loans serviced under the Special Servicing option.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Regular Servicing Option
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Under the Regular Servicing option (identified by foreclosure risk code L), the lender assumes the entire risk of loss from a borrower default while servicing the mortgage and, as a result, is charged a lower guaranty fee. The lender also agrees to provide for a successor to this agreement if it transfers its responsibilities for servicing the mortgage to another servicer.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Special Servicing Option
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Under the Special Servicing option (identified by foreclosure risk code F), Fannie Mae assumes the risk of loss from a borrower default. Fannie Mae reimburses the lender for its share of the lender's unrecovered advances for delinquencies and advances related to servicing the mortgages.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See the Servicing Guide for more information about both options.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-2-03, MBS Remittance Type and Selecting a Remittance Cycle
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: In this topic, the lender's options with respect to remitting principal and interest on MBS pool loans are addressed, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Pool Remittance Type
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Standard Remittance Cycle
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Pool Remittance Type
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All MBS mortgages are accounted for as the scheduled/scheduled remittance type. This means that a servicer may have to advance its own funds to cover the amount Fannie Mae is due on a mortgage for which the monthly payment of principal and interest is late.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standard Remittance Cycle
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Under the standard remittance cycle, lenders remit scheduled P&I payments on the 18th of the month in which they are due from the borrowers (or, if the 18th is not a business day, on the business day preceding the 18th). Unscheduled principal payments must be remitted by the 18th of the month following collection.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-2-04, Mandatory MBS Commitments (12/06/2016)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on obtaining and fulfilling mandatory commitments to sell loans under an MBS execution, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Mandatory MBS Committing Process
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Access to Guaranty Fee Pricing
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Negotiated Commitments
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: MBS Commitment Rolls and Pair-Offs
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A lender must obtain an MBS commitment before it can deliver mortgages into a single lender MBS pool or a Fannie Majors. The commitment evidences an agreement between Fannie Mae and a lender to buy and sell, respectively, mortgages for inclusion in a particular MBS pool. These contracts set forth the terms and conditions for delivery of specified mortgages for MBS. When a lender enters into a mandatory MBS commitment to sell loans to Fannie Mae, the lender agrees to sell a certain volume of loans having a specified set of loan parameters to Fannie Mae, and Fannie Mae provides the lender with guaranty fee pricing for such mortgage loans for delivery under MBS execution.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Mandatory MBS Committing Process
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: To deliver MBS loans to Fannie Mae a lender must obtain a mandatory MBS commitment using Fannie Mae's MBS committing application, a free Web-based application available to subscribers approved for MBS execution. The lender's agreement to use Pricing & Execution (PE)-MBS in accordance with the applicable requirements is part of the Lender Contract. For more information and terms of use applicable to the PE-MBS application, see Fannie Mae's website.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders can obtain commitments on any weekday, with the exception of bond market observed holidays as defined by the Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association (SIFMA). Lenders can also obtain commitments for delivery in a future MBS issue month for all mortgage products for which pricing is offered through the MBS committing application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae confirmed its acceptance of the lender's request for a mandatory commitment by electronically transmitting a commitment confirmation to the lender. Lenders should contact the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk if they experience any problems receiving a commitment confirmation (see E-1-02, List of Contacts).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Access to Guaranty Fee Pricing
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders obtain current guaranty fee pricing specific to their institution by accessing Fannie Mae's MBS committing application. If the lender wants to request pricing for a mortgage product in an issue month that is not available in the MBS committing application, then the lender must contact its Fannie Mae customer account team or the Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Negotiated Commitments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders that want to sell loans to Fannie Mae that contain unique eligibility and underwriting considerations not permissible for delivery via a standard commitment may request a negotiated commitment. Lenders should contact their Fannie Mae customer account team for details.
elements.label: text

elements.text: MBS Commitment Rolls and Pair-Offs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: MBS commitments are for mandatory delivery by the lender. If the lender is unable to meet the terms of the commitment, the lender must take the action in the MBS committing application to pair-off or roll the outstanding balance of the commitment before the last business day of the issue month applicable to that commitment. Fannie Mae may charge lenders for lender-executed or lender-requested rolls or pair-offs for mandatory commitments. These fees, if charged, are drafted from the lender's designated account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Fannie Mae may pair-off the outstanding balance of a commitment on a lender's behalf if the lender fails to pairoff or roll the outstanding balance into the following issue month by last business day of the issue month of the related commitment.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.

elements.text: Chapter C3-3, MBS Guaranty Fees and Guaranty Fee Buyups and Buydowns
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must pay Fannie Mae a guaranty fee remittance each month as compensation for the lender's right to participate in the MBS program. This chapter provides information on guaranty fees and the requirements associated with buying the fee up or down.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-3-01, Determining and Remitting Guaranty Fees (10/25/2011)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This topic provides information on the factors used to determine the amount of a lender's guaranty fee, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Guaranty Fee Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Annualized Weighted-Average Guaranty Fee Factor for a Pool
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Determining the Monthly Guaranty Fee Remittance for the Pool
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Remitting Guaranty Fees to Fannie Mae
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Guaranty Fee Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The guaranty fee for individual loans included in an MBS pool depends upon the credit risk associated with the loan and the lender's choice of servicing option and remittance cycle. The guaranty fee remittance for MBS pools is based upon a weighted-average guaranty factor. See below for a description of how to calculate the annualized weighted-guaranty factor and the pool guaranty fee.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Annualized Weighted-Average Guaranty Fee Factor for a Pool
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for a pool (expressed in basis points) is determined by multiplying the issue date principal balance of each mortgage loan in the pool (or, in subsequent months, the
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: 'scheduled' balance of each mortgage loan) by the applicable guaranty fee rate for the mortgage loan expressed in basis points (adjusted for any remittance cycle adjustments, buyup, or buydown, if applicable), then:
elements.label: text

elements.text: summing the products of all the mortgage loans in the pool,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: dividing that sum by the aggregate issue date principal balance for the pool (or, in subsequent months, by the 'scheduled' security balance for the pool), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: rounding the result to the nearest hundredth of a basis point.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The result is the annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor for a pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Determining the Monthly Guaranty Fee Remittance for the Pool
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The annualized weighted-average guaranty fee factor is then
elements.label: text

elements.text: multiplied by the aggregate issue date principal balance of the pool (or, in subsequent months, the 'scheduled' security balance for the pool),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: divided by 120,000, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: rounded to two decimal places.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The result is monthly guaranty fee remittance for the pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Remitting Guaranty Fees to Fannie Mae
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: On the 7th day of each month (or, if the 7th is a holiday or weekend, on the preceding business day), Fannie Mae will draft guaranty fee remittances from the custodial bank account the lender designates as its draft account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the guaranty fee remittance is to be drafted from the lender's P&I custodial account for MBS pools, then the lender must make sure that it does not withdraw the guaranty fee remittance when it takes its monthly servicing fee from the account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For more information about designating bank accounts for drafting purposes and confirming guaranty fee remittances, see the Servicing Guide .
elements.label: text

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: C3-3-02, Accessing Buyup and Buydown Ratios and Calculating Payments or Charges (12/04/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information lenders need in order to buy up or buy down their guaranty fee, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Information on Buyups
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Information on Buydowns
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Accessing Buyup and Buydown Ratios
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Calculating Buyup Payments and Buydown Charges
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Buyup Payment Recapture
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Information on Buyups
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Loan level guaranty fee buyups and buydowns allow lenders to pool a wider range of note rates under one MBS coupon. Lenders can buy up guaranty fees, meaning they agree to remit a guaranty fee higher than the contractual fee applicable for the particular servicing option and remittance cycle in return for a one-time payment from Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae disburses payment for buyups to lenders once a month in a single cash transfer covering all of the lender's mortgages with buyups that were in pools for which securities were issued in the previous month.
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Information on Buydowns
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders can buy down or agree to remit a lower guaranty fee than the applicable contract fee in exchange for a one-time, upfront payment to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When Fannie Mae drafts the lender's monthly guaranty fee remittances, it also will draft charges for buydowns on pooled loans for which securities were issued in the previous month. Generally, lenders must place buydown funds into a T&I escrow account. If buydown funds (or subsidy payments or other advance payments made by the borrower) are directed for immediate application under the mortgage documents, the lender may deposit these amounts directly into the P&I custodial account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a lender holds MBS certificates and the buydown account and its attorneys believe there are tax complications, the lender may deposit the funds in a special account with another institution that meets Fannie Mae's requirements for custodial depositories. For more information, see Fannie Mae's Servicing Guide .
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a lender buys down a guaranty fee but does not service the associated mortgage, Fannie Mae establishes a receivable in the lender's name. The lender must deposit the funds into a designated account in time for Fannie Mae to draft the funds on the seventh calendar day of the month (or the preceding business day if the seventh is a holiday or weekend) following the issue date of the related MBS pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Accessing Buyup and Buydown Ratios
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Fannie Mae posts guaranty fee buyup/buydown ratios for nearly all mortgage products in a matrix format that enables a lender to find the applicable buyup or buydown ratio for any individual mortgage that it plans to include in an MBS pool by using the gross note rate and remaining term (in months) of the mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Information concerning guaranty fee buyup or buydown ratios can come from several different sources. For example,
elements.label: text

elements.text: registered users of PE-MBS can obtain them on the day they are posted by accessing PE-MBS, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: lenders may request a copy of the posted ratios from their Fannie Mae customer account team.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Refer to Fannie Mae's website for information about the buyup and buydown grids.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Calculating Buyup Payments and Buydown Charges
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The total buyup disbursement or buydown charge is calculated as follows:
elements.label: text

elements.text: multiply the number of basis points by which each mortgage in a given pool was bought up/down (the difference between the 'guaranty fee rate before the buyup or buydown' and the 'guaranty fee rate after the buyup/buydown') by the 'buyup/buydown per basis point' (rounding to three decimal places),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: multiply the rounded product by 0.0001 to convert the basis points to a decimal equivalent, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: multiply the decimal equivalent by the delivered unpaid principal balance with a guaranty fee rate buyup/buydown.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Do this for all pools that include mortgages with guaranty fee rate buyups/buydowns and settled in the month. The total of the buyups or buydowns for all of the pools will be the amount Fannie Mae collects, disburses, or nets.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Buyup Payment Recapture
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: With respect to any mortgage loan that pays off within 120 days from the MBS issue date that involved a guaranty fee buyup, Fannie Mae reserves the right to request reimbursement of any or all buyup proceeds paid by Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For mortgage loans repurchased by a lender, Fannie Mae in its sole discretion may require reimbursement by the lender of any buyup proceeds paid by Fannie Mae in connection with the purchase of the related repurchased mortgage loan without regard to the 120-day limitation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements for ARM Guaranty Fee Buyups and Buydowns
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender may buy up or buy down the guaranty fee remittance for MBS pools consisting of adjustable-rate mortgages (ARMs) originated under any standard ARM plan, as long as the mortgages meet the following eligibility criteria:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The guaranty fee rate may be bought up or bought down in increments of 0.0001% (one one-hundredth of a basis point).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Any buyup or buydown of the guaranty fee remittance rate for adjustable-rate MBS pools must be calculated on the reduced guaranty fee remittance rate that applies to this type of remittance cycle.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Guaranty Fee Buyup Maximums and Buydown Minimums for ARMs
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The weighted-average coupon of the pooled mortgages must not exceed the following limits:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 1.000% (100 basis points) higher than the accrual rate (the pass-through rate) for the pool as of the issue date for mortgages originated under most ARM plans; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 0.875% (87.5 basis points for mortgages originated under ARM plans that have initial fixed-rate periods of 3, 5, 7, or 10 years).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The recommended maximum allowable interest rate is 100 basis points (1%) over the minimum allowable interest rate.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Additionally, for all ARM plans, the guaranty fee buyup is limited to 0.25% (25 basis points). The guaranty fee cannot be bought down to a number less than zero.

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders can pool mortgages into one of two types of MBS: fixed-rate MBS and ARM MBS. This chapter provides information on pooling loans into fixed-rate MBS, which includes UMBS.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-4-01, Term-Related Fixed-Rate Mortgage Pooling Parameters (05/01/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae issues and specially designates securities backed by either government-insured or governmentguaranteed loans or conventional loans. This topic contains information on pooling fixed-rate mortgages (FRM) of various terms, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 30-year (Long-Term) FRM MBS / UMBS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 20-year FRM MBS / UMBS

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 30-year (Long-Term) FRM MBS / UMBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Each loan in a long-term fixed-rate MBS pool must provide for constant monthly installments payable on a specified day of the month of an amount sufficient to pay interest and amortize the principal amount of the loan within the remaining term.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: The loans must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: have original terms of no less than 181 months and no more than 360 months for both single-issuer pools and Fannie Majors, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provide for the reduction of the principal amount of the loan to zero no more than 30 years after the issue date of the related securities.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Loans that provide for interest to be paid in advance may be included in an MBS pool without converting the interest payment to interest in arrears on a loan-by-loan basis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 20-year FRM MBS / UMBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae issues and specially designates securities that are backed by conventional loans that have loan terms that do not extend for more than 20 years after the origination of the loan. Such securities will have a maturity date no greater than 20 years from their issue date, which represents the date by which all loans backing the pool will have been liquidated from the pool and the securities will have been retired.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The loans must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: have original terms of no less than 181 months and no more than 240 months for both single-issuer pools and Fannie Majors, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provide for the reduction of the principal amount of the loan to zero no more than 20 years after the issue date of the related securities.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 15-year (Intermediate-Term) FRM MBS / UMBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae issues and specially designates securities that are backed by conventional loans that have loan terms that do not extend for more than 15 years after the origination of the loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Such securities will have a maturity date no greater than 15 years from their issue date, which represents the date by which all loans backing the pool will have been liquidated from the pool and the securities will have been retired.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The loans must:
elements.label: text

elements.text: have original terms of no less than 85 months and no more than 180 months for both single-issuer pools and Fannie Majors, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provide for the reduction of the principal amount of the loan to zero no more than 15 years after the issue date of the related securities.
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: page_footer

Such securities will have a maturity date no greater than 10 years from their issue date, which represents the date by which all loans backing the pool will have been liquidated from the pool and the securities will have been retired.

elements.text: Chapter C3-5, Pooling Loans into ARM MBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are two primary types of ARM MBS pools with weighted-average coupons, also called ARM Flex pools. Accrual rates for ARM Flex pools may be based on a weighted-average or a fixed MBS margin. This chapter provides information on pooling loans into ARM MBS, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-5-01, Creating Weighted-Average ARM MBS (08/26/2014)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on creating weighted-average ARM MBS (ARM Flex), including
elements.label: text

elements.text: General Information on Weighted-Average ARM MBS (ARM Flex)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ARM Flex Pool Accrual Rate Calculation Options
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: General Information on Weighted-Average ARM MBS (ARM Flex)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: ARM Flex pools provide interest accruals at a weighted-average pool accrual rate to the holders of securities backed by the ARMs in the pool. These pools may be delivered only as single pool transactions and different standard ARM plans may not be commingled in the same pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The original term of an ARM included in a weighted-average ARM MBS pool must be no more than 30 years. Each mortgage must accrue interest in arrears and have a payment due date of the first day of the month. There is no restriction on the range of first payment due dates, interest rate change dates, and payment change dates.
elements.label: text

elements.text: To limit the effect that prepayments have on the weighted-average pool accrual rate of an ARM Flex, lenders should consider limiting to 1% (100 basis points) the range between the lowest and highest mortgage margins and the lowest and highest mortgage interest rate ceilings of all the mortgages in the pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: ARM Flex Pool Accrual Rate Calculation Options
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Under the ARM Flex pooling structure, lenders may create a weighted-average ARM MBS using either the
elements.label: text

elements.text: fixed MBS margin or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: weighted-average MBS margin.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The difference between the two margin types is the method used to determine the interest rate that accrues on the pool and the retained servicing fee. The fixed MBS margin option is based on a pool-level MBS margin and a loan-level servicing fee. This means that when the pooled mortgages have different mortgage margins, the servicing fees for the mortgages vary in order to equalize the differences in the mortgage margins. While the guaranty fee remains the same, the servicing spread (the sum of the servicing fee and the guaranty fee and, if applicable the periodic renewal premium for lender-purchased mortgage insurance) differs from mortgage to mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The fixed MBS margin applies to each mortgage in the weighted-average ARM MBS; therefore, the lowest mortgage margin in the pool must be able to, at least, support the sum of the MBS margin, the guaranty fee, and the minimum allowable servicing fee. The periodic renewal premium for lender-purchased mortgage insurance must also be included, if applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The weighted-average MBS margin option is based on a pool-level servicing fee and a loan-level MBS margin, which means that when the mortgages in the pool have different mortgage margins, the servicing spread will be equal for all the mortgages, but the MBS margin will vary from mortgage to mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-5-04, Pooling ARMs with a Conversion Option (05/01/2019)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information on pooling ARMs with conversion options, including
elements.label: text

elements.text: ARM Conversion Options
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Redelivering a Converted ARM that Originally Required Recourse or Credit Enhancement
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ARM Conversion Options
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a borrower chooses to convert an adjustable-rate mortgage that includes an option to convert to a fixed-rate mortgage, the lender must repurchase the mortgage from the MBS pool before interest begins accruing at the new fixed rate. Lenders may choose from two options for disposing of the converted mortgage after it has been repurchased from the pool:
elements.label: text

elements.text: take-out option
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: market rate option
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the take-out post-conversion option was selected when the convertible ARM was delivered, the servicer must redeliver the converted mortgage to Fannie Mae as a whole loan or as part of a fixed-rate MBS (A/A remittance type only).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The take-out option is not available on mortgages that have lender-purchased mortgage insurance or were originated under an ARM plan that permits conversion to a fixed interest rate only on specified interest rate adjustment dates.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The conversion option selected for each convertible ARM delivered to Fannie Mae must be identified by the appropriate special feature code:
elements.label: text

elements.text: SFC 037 Convertible ARM - Take-Out Option, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: SFC 038 Convertible ARM - Market Rate Option.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In order to receive a par price on a redelivered converted mortgage, the lender must obtain a take-out mandatory delivery commitment (Converted ARM Resale Commitment) by the 16th day of the month in which the conversion was requested.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the market rate option was chosen, the servicer is not required to redeliver the mortgage to Fannie Mae, although it may choose to redeliver the mortgage as a whole loan (A/A, S/A or S/S remittance types) or as part of a fixed-rate MBS. It may deliver the loan under any existing standard fixed-rate whole loan or MBS
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: commitment that it has outstanding, as long as Fannie Mae's original acceptance of the mortgage was not conditional on the lender providing some type of special recourse or credit enhancement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Redelivering a Converted ARM that Originally Required Recourse or Credit Enhancement
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If Fannie Mae originally required recourse or credit enhancement on an ARM that includes an option to convert to a fixed-rate mortgage, and the conditions that led to its requirement still exist, then lenders will not be able to use a standard commitment to redeliver the converted mortgage to Fannie Mae. Instead, lenders must
elements.label: text

elements.text: obtain a new negotiated contract (which may or may not require recourse or credit enhancement) or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: include the converted mortgage as part of a delivery under an outstanding negotiated contract that has acceptable recourse or credit enhancement.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the conditions that led to Fannie Mae requiring recourse or credit enhancement no longer exist, and Fannie Mae determines that the recourse or credit enhancement is no longer necessary, lenders may redeliver the converted mortgage under
elements.label: text

elements.text: a standard whole loan commitment, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a standard MBS commitment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: C3-5-05, Commingling ARMs in MBS (02/23/2016)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic provides information on commingling ARMs, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: ARM Loan Types Lenders May Commingle in the Same Pool
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ARM Loan Types Lenders May Not Commingle in the Same Pool
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ARM Loan Types Lenders May Commingle in the Same Pool
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: ARMs originated under the same ARM plan may have original terms up to 30 years and may be commingled in the same ARM pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: ARM Loan Types Lenders May Not Commingle in the Same Pool
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: ARMs originated under different ARM plans cannot be commingled in the same pool. In certain cases, however, lenders may be permitted to pool seasoned ARMs in ARM plans with shorter initial fixed-rate periods. Interested lenders should contact their Fannie Mae customer account team to determine what flexibility may be available for a specific situation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: ARMs originated under a single ARM plan, but with different post-conversion disposition options (market rate option or take-out option) cannot be included in the same pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter C3-6, Pooling Loans into Fannie Majors
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders can pool mortgages into two types of MBS, which includes UMBS: Single-Issuer and Fannie Majors. This chapter provides information on pooling loans into Fannie Majors.
elements.label: text

elements.text: C3-6-01, Parameters for Pooling Loans Into Fannie Majors (09/04/2024)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains general parameters for pooling loans into Fannie Majors, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Majors Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Obtaining Fannie Majors Pool Numbers
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Selecting Mortgages to Include in a Fannie Majors Pool
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Selecting a Settlement Date and Security Issuance
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Making Multiple Submissions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Majors Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Majors are pools of loans that are contributed by more than one lender. Lenders participating in a Fannie Majors pool receive an MBS representing its share of the pool in proportion to the dollar amount of mortgages it contributed to the pool.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Obtaining Fannie Majors Pool Numbers
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The pool number identifies the pools in which a lender's loans are included. Fannie Mae assigns Fannie Majors pool numbers based on loan term, pass-through rate, and the month of issuance. Pool numbers, CUSIP numbers, and pass-through rates are posted on the Fannie Majors page on Fannie Mae's website and on the various wire services.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Selecting Mortgages to Include in a Fannie Majors Pool
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When deciding which mortgages to include in a Fannie Majors pool, lenders must make sure that:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the mortgage conforms to the appropriate product type,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the mortgage has the appropriate seasoning,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the note rate is within the minimum and maximum range in relation to the pass-through rate, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the minimum submission size is satisfied.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: To be eligible for a Fannie Majors pool, mortgages may be no older than 11 months as of the security issue date. For example, all loans to be included in a December 2019 pool, must have first payment dates on or after January 2019.
elements.label: text

elements.text: As with single-lender, fixed-rate MBS pools, the minimum and maximum allowable interest rate within a Fannie Majors pool is 25 basis points (.25%) and 112.5 basis points (1.125%) respectively above the pool's passthrough rate. Lenders can use loan-level buyups and buydowns to eliminate excess servicing and, in the case of buydowns, include loans in a higher coupon.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Majors have a minimum submission requirement of $1,000 when lenders deliver the loan to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: To qualify for a Fannie Mae Green Majors pool, loans must meet Green MBS eligibility standards and have an eligible green building certification. See  C1-2-05, Delivering Green MBS to Fannie Mae for additional details.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Selecting a Settlement Date and Security Issuance
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders may choose their own settlement date for a Fannie Majors pool. If a lender makes several submissions into the same Fannie Majors pool, it may select different settlement dates for each.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae can issue securities in book-entry form within three business days after the lender submits a complete, error-free pool documentation package via Loan Delivery, as long as Fannie Mae receives:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the lender's transmission of the loan delivery data by 6:00 p.m. (Eastern time), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the transmission of the document custodian's pool certification by 10:30 a.m. (Eastern time) on the day following the lender's Loan Delivery transmission.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See C3-7-06, Settling the Trade, for additional information on turnaround times.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: A lender delivering into Fannie Majors receives securities rounded to the lowest whole dollar amount of the loans it contributed to the pool, unless it requests otherwise. Fannie Mae issues certificates in minimum denominations of $1,000 with additional increments of one dollar. Only one security denomination can be issued for each account.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Making Multiple Submissions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender may deliver several different pieces for inclusion in a multiple pool (Fannie Majors) as long as all of the pieces have the same remittance cycle. This means that delivery of multiple servicer numbers per seller number is permitted in a Fannie Majors pool; however each piece must have one seller/servicer combination. In addition, each piece must be submitted separately and must meet the $1,000 minimum submission requirement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae will combine the submissions with the same branch seller/servicer number and remittance day into the same pool under the same lender. These loans must be serviced as one pool after issuance. The dollar amount of the lender's securities will be equal to the sum of the issue date principal balances of all the mortgages from each of the lender's pool documentation packages related to the multiple pool, rounded down to the next lowest whole dollar amount.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders may deliver nonstandard loans into Fannie Majors MBS pools. Each discrete delivery the lender makes under a given Fannie Majors TBA-eligible UMBS pool will be subject to the limitations that apply to a single TBA-eligible UMBS pool. See  C3-2-01, Determining Eligibility for Loans Pooled into MBS for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter C3-7, Delivering and Trading MBS
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders that sell their MBS rather than hold them as an investment may work with Fannie Mae's Capital Markets Pricing and Sales Desk to obtain a competitive price. In this chapter, the steps entailed in delivering and trading MBS and the requirements associated with these processes are provided.

elements.label: text

elements.label: text
